title: Home
description: Phrasal verbs about home and household activities

points.definition: To make a device, machine, or system start working

examples.english: Can you turn on the TV?
Turn it on, please. I want to watch the news.

points.notes: Separable: 'Turn the TV on' or 'Turn on the TV'. With pronouns, MUST separate: 'Turn it on' (NOT 'turn on it').

points.definition: To make a device, machine, or system stop working

examples.english: Please turn off the lights before you leave.
Don't forget to turn the oven off!

points.notes: Opposite of 'turn on'. Same separable rules apply.

points.definition: To lift something from a surface using your hands

examples.english: Can you pick up that book from the floor?
She picked up the phone and called her mum.

points.notes: Also means to collect someone: 'I'll pick you up at 8'.

points.definition: To put something back in its proper place

examples.english: Please put away your toys before dinner.
I need to put the dishes away.

points.notes: Commonly used by parents with children. Keep things tidy!

points.title: Practice: Home
points.description: Complete the exercises with turn on, turn off, pick up, and put away

questions.sentence: It's cold in here. Can you ______ ______ the heating?
questions.feedback: Correct! 'Turn on' means to activate a device or system.

questions.sentence: I dropped my keys. Can you pick them ______?
questions.feedback: Yes! 'Pick up' means to lift something from a surface.

questions.instruction: Now replace 'the radio' with the pronoun 'it'.
questions.feedback: With pronouns, separable phrasal verbs MUST separate: 'Turn it off'.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.feedback: With pronouns, the object goes between the verb and particle: 'Turn it off'.
Great! 'Put away' your clothes means to tidy them up.

questions.context: A: It's very dark in this room. B: Let me ______ ______ the lights.
questions.feedback: Perfect! 'Turn on the lights' to make the room brighter.
